questions.title: Variable Scope
questions.explanation: Local variable x in func() shadows global x. func() prints 20, main() prints global x = 10. Variable scope determines where a variable can be accessed. Local variables are only accessible within their block.
hints: Remember that local variables shadow globals
Remember that each x is in different scope
questions.hint: Think about: local variable x in func() shadows global x.

questions.title: Block Scope
questions.explanation: Block inside if creates new scope. Inner x=10 shadows outer x=5 within block. Outside block, outer x=5 is used. Variable scope determines where a variable can be accessed. Local variables are only accessible within their block.
hints: Remember that blocks create new scope
Remember that inner x shadows outer
Remember that after block, outer x visible
questions.hint: Think about variable scope and lifetime in C.

questions.title: Type Cast
questions.explanation: Casting double to int truncates (doesn't round). 9.7 becomes 9. Type casting in C converts a value from one type to another. Casting happens before the operation when you cast an operand.
questions.hint: Remember that when both operands are integers, C performs integer division which truncates the decimal part.

questions.title: Logical AND Short-circuit

questions.title: Assert Statement

questions.title: NDEBUG Macro

questions.explanation: NDEBUG disables all assertions. assert(0) is removed, program continues normally.

questions.explanation: Duff's device is an advanced loop unrolling technique that combines a switch statement with a loop to optimize memory copy operations by reducing loop overhead.

questions.title: Infinite For Loop
questions.description: Which of these creates an infinite loop?
questions.explanation: A: for(;;) is infinite (no condition means always true). B: Missing condition means always true. C: i never increments but will eventually be optimized or timeout, but technically finite if broken. A and B are infinite.
hints: Remember that missing condition defaults to true
Think about for(;;) is classic infinite loop
Remember that c doesn't increment but has a condition

questions.title: Loop Unrolling Benefit

questions.explanation: Loop unrolling reduces the number of iterations, decreasing loop overhead (increment, comparison, branch) and improving CPU pipeline efficiency.
hints: Remember that fewer loop control operations
Remember that better CPU pipeline utilization

questions.title: Left Shift Operation
questions.explanation: Left shifting by n positions multiplies by 2^n. 5 << 2 = 5 * 4 = 20. In binary: 101 << 2 = 10100 = 20.
hints: Remember that left shift is equivalent to multiplying by powers of 2
Remember that each shift left doubles the value
questions.hint: Think about: left shifting by n positions multiplies by 2^n.

questions.title: Right Shift Operation
questions.explanation: Right shifting by n positions divides by 2^n (for positive integers). 20 >> 2 = 20 / 4 = 5. In binary: 10100 >> 2 = 101 = 5.
hints: Remember that right shift is equivalent to dividing by powers of 2
Remember that each shift right halves the value
questions.hint: Think about: right shifting by n positions divides by 2^n (for positive integers).

questions.title: Arithmetic Right Shift with Negative Numbers
questions.explanation: For signed integers, right shift is arithmetic (sign-extending). -16 >> 2 = -4. The sign bit is preserved, filling leftmost bits with 1s.
hints: Remember that arithmetic right shift preserves the sign
Remember that sign bits are copied into the vacated positions
Remember that this maintains the negative value
questions.hint: Think about: for signed integers, right shift is arithmetic (sign-extending).

questions.title: Setting a Specific Bit
questions.explanation: To set bit n, use x | (1 << n). Here: 8 (1000) | 2 (0010) = 10 (1010). Bit 1 is now set.
hints: Remember that oR operation can set bits without clearing others
Think about 1 << 1 creates a mask with only bit 1 set
The solution involves 8 in binary is 1000
questions.hint: Think about: to set bit n, use x | (1 << n).

questions.title: Toggling a Specific Bit
questions.explanation: To toggle bit n, use x ^ (1 << n). Here: 10 (1010) ^ 4 (0100) = 14 (1110). Bit 2 flipped from 0 to 1.
hints: Remember that xOR flips bits: 0 becomes 1, 1 becomes 0
Think about 1 << 2 creates a mask with only bit 2 set
questions.hint: Think about: to toggle bit n, use x ^ (1 << n).

questions.title: Integer Overflow with Left Shift
questions.explanation: 1 << 31 sets the most significant bit of a 32-bit unsigned int. This equals 2^31 = 2147483648. No overflow occurs with unsigned types.
hints: Remember that left shift by n multiplies by 2^n
Remember that unsigned integers wrap around on overflow
questions.hint: Think about: 1 << 31 sets the most significant bit of a 32-bit unsigned int.

questions.title: XOR for Swapping
questions.explanation: XOR swap trick: after the three operations, a and b are swapped without a temporary variable. Final result: a=7, b=5.
hints: Remember that this is a classic XOR swap technique
Remember that xOR properties allow swapping without temp variable
Remember that trace through each step carefully
questions.hint: Think about: xor swap trick: after the three operations, a and b are swapped without a temporary variable.

questions.title: Shift Amount Greater Than Width
questions.description: What is the behavior of this code?
questions.explanation: Shifting by an amount greater than or equal to the width of the type is undefined behavior in C. On a 32-bit int, shifting by 32 is undefined. Undefined behavior means the C standard doesn't specify what should happen. Avoid it by following language rules carefully.
hints: Remember that check the bit width of the type
Remember that shifting by width or more is problematic
Remember that the C standard leaves this undefined
questions.hint: Remember that this code exhibits undefined behavior. Consider what C standard says about this operation.
